summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier Software Update article with reader feedback and corrections. He addresses two main debates: whether Repair Permissions should be run as routine maintenance (he says no, it's a troubleshooting tool), and whether combo updaters are superior to delta updaters (he says Software Update handles it correctly). He also corrects his claim about the iPod Shuffle being Apple's first price-per-megabyte leader by noting the Xserve RAID, and observes Apple confusingly reusing the term 'dashboard' for both Tiger's widget layer and editing palettes in Motion and iPhoto 5.
key_points: Repair Permissions is a troubleshooting tool, not routine maintenance — if permissions keep changing, something deeper is wrong with your installation
Delta vs. combo updaters should be a non-issue for users who run Software Update regularly, as it automatically selects the right one
Users who mix and match system files between OS revisions create unsupported configurations and then blame the updaters when things break
The Xserve RAID was another Apple product that competed favorably on cost-per-megabyte, contradicting Gruber's iPod Shuffle claim
Apple is confusingly using 'dashboard' to describe both Tiger's widget layer and editing palettes in Motion and iPhoto 5
money_quotes: File permissions and ownership don't have a half-life — they don't rot or 'go bad' or even change over time. If they've changed, some software had to have changed them.
My guess is that most of the people who run into problems with delta updaters have diddled with files they ought not have diddled.
This is madness. I'm not denying that this worked for Mr. Marks, but what he's ended up with is a completely unsupported system configuration.
Apple designs and tests the delta updaters to work against the standard installation of the previous OS revision — once you've started diddling with system files, all bets are off.
Mixing and matching individual files between OS revisions is a recipe for disaster, although the disaster might not occur immediately.
In short, if you use Software Update — and I recommend that you do — the Right Thing should just happen.
key_insight: Most Mac OS X maintenance superstitions — routine Repair Permissions, mandatory combo updaters — are unnecessary for users who keep standard installations and let Software Update do its job.
